findings.title: CORS Wildcard Origin in Production Template
findings.description: The SAM template defaults the CORS Access-Control-Allow-Origin header to wildcard ('*'), which allows any website to make authenticated requests to the API Gateway endpoints.
findings.exploit_scenario:
1. Attacker creates malicious website at evil.com
2. Legitimate user visits evil.com while logged into WebAuthnKit
3. Malicious JavaScript makes authenticated requests to WebAuthnKit API
4. Browser allows cross-origin request due to CORS wildcard
5. Attacker reads credentials, modifies user data, or deletes account
findings.impact: Complete compromise of user account data via CSRF from any origin
findings.recommendation: Change default CORS origin to actual frontend URL: APICorsOrigin: Default: "'https://your-react-app-domain.com'"
findings.playbook:
1. Update backend/template.yaml parameter default
2. Redeploy SAM stack
3. Verify CORS headers in browser devtools
4. Test that unauthorized origins are blocked

findings.title: Unpinned Third-Party GitHub Actions
findings.description: GitHub Actions are pinned to @master instead of SHA hashes, allowing action maintainers or attackers who compromise action repos to inject malicious code.
findings.exploit_scenario:
1. Attacker compromises snyk/actions or actions/checkout repo
2. Pushes malicious code to master branch
3. Next WebAuthnKit CI run pulls compromised @master
4. Malicious action extracts SNYKTOKEN or modifies artifacts
5. Attacker gains Snyk access or backdoors Lambda functions
findings.impact: CI/CD compromise leading to secret exfiltration or backdoored deployments
findings.recommendation: Pin all third-party actions to full commit SHAs with version comments. Use Dependabot for updates.
findings.playbook:
1. For each action, find latest stable release SHA
2. Replace @master with @{sha} # v{version}
3. Enable Dependabot for GitHub Actions
4. Commit and push

findings.title: Missing CODEOWNERS Protection on Workflows
findings.description: No CODEOWNERS file to enforce review requirements on workflow modifications. Any contributor can modify CI/CD without security review.
findings.exploit_scenario:
1. External contributor submits PR with code changes
2. PR includes subtle workflow modification (e.g., secret exfiltration)
3. Maintainer reviews code but misses workflow change
4. PR merged, workflow runs with attacker's changes
5. Secrets exfiltrated or malicious code deployed
findings.impact: Unauthorized workflow changes enabling CI/CD compromise
findings.playbook:
1. Create .github/CODEOWNERS
2. Add workflow protection rule
3. Enable branch protection requiring CODEOWNERS approval
4. Commit and push
